content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
February 16,
1994
Gary T.
Galliano
City Attorney
City of Union City
34009 Alvarado-Niles Road
Union City,
CA 94587
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—94—024
Dear Mr.
Galliano:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Maggie Matthews,
a member of the Union City Mobile
Home
Space Rent Review Board,
regarding her responsibilities under the
conflict—of—interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the
“Act”).
1
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTION
May Commissioner Matthews participate in the consideration of
an application to raise rents submitted by the Tropics Mobile Home
Park,
the park at which she and her spouse rent
a mobile home
space?
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18954.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
A-94-024
Page
2
CONCLUS ION
Pursuant to Regulation 18703.3, the commissioner may
participate in the consideration of the application to raise rents
submitted by the Tropics Mobile Home Park so long as the decision
will affect 10 percent or more of the tenants of mobile home parks
in the jurisdiction in substantially the same manner.
FACT S
Commissioner Matthews was appointed to the Union City Mobile
Home Space Rent Review Board (the “board”) pursuant to Section
16.40.050(c)
of the Union City Municipal Code to represent mobile
home park tenants.
Commissioner Matthews and her spouse
own a
mobile home in Union City and rent a space for the mobile home
(on
a month to month basis)
in a mobile home park called the Tropics
Mobile Home Park.
Of the 16,576 dwelling units in the city,
933
are in mobile home parks and 544 are in the Tropics Mobile Home
Park.
2
You anticipate that the Tropics Mobile Home Park will apply
to the board for a rent increase.
You stated that the effect of
the increase will be to increase the commissioner’s rent by as
much as $165 per month
(from the existing rent of $350.95 per
month).
You have asked whether the commissioner may participate
in the decision.
ANALYSIS
Economic Interests
Section 87100 provides that no public official at any level
of state or local government shall make, participate in making,
or
in any way attempt to use his or her official position to
influence a governmental decision in which the official knows or
has reason to know he or she has a financial interest.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family,
or on:
Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one
thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
Section 87103(b).
2
In communication of February 15,
1994,
you provided revised
figures regarding the number of mobile home spaces in the Tropics
Mobile Home Park
(544 mobile homes)
and the number city wide
(933
mobile homes).


Our File No.
A-94-024
Page
3
Commissioner Matthews owns a mobile home in the jurisdiction
which is located on property rented from the Tropics Mobile Home
Park on a month—to—month basis.
The Act defines an “interest in
real property” to include any leasehold interest in real property
located in the jurisdiction,
but expressly excludes month—to—month
tenancies from this definition.
(Section 82033; Regulation
18233.)
However,
Section 87103
and Regulation 18702.1(a) (4 provide
that decisions which will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on the official’s personal expenses,
income,
assets,
or liabilities are also potentially disqualifying.
The
effect of a decision on a public official’s personal expenses,
income, assets,
or liabilities is material if the decisions will
result in the increase or decrease of the expenses,
income,
or
liabilities,
or the value of assets by at least $250.
(Regulation
18702.1(a) (4).)
The increase or decrease in the rent on the commissioner’s
space would appear to have an effect on the fair market value of
the mobile home.
(Jorgensen Advice Letter,
No. A-90-017.)
If the
effect will be $250 or more Commissioner Matthews would be
required to disqualify herself from the decision.
In addition, the commissioner must also disqualify herself if
any action of the board would increase or decrease her liabilities
or expenses by
$250 or more.
For example,
approval of
a rent
increase which would increase a public official’s rent liability
(along with other mobile home park renters)
by $250 would require
the official to disqualify himself or herself.
(Flitner Advice
Letter,
No.
1-92-065.)
Under your facts,
the increase in the rent
on an annual basis would be $1,980,
far in excess of the
$250
threshold.
Thus,
the commissioner is prohibited from
participating in the rent adjustment decision.
“Public Generally” Exception
Regulation 18703 provides an exception to the conflict-of-
interest provisions if the effect on the official’s interest is
not distinguishable from the effect on the public generally.
For
the “public generally” exception to apply,
a decision must affect
the official’s interests in substantially the same manner as it
would affect a significant segment of the public.
(Regulation
18703.)
Whether the financial consequences of
a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends
on
the facts of each particular case.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is
a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is not required.
However,
if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable.
(In
re Thorner
(1975)
1
FPPC Ops.
198.)


Our File No.
A—94-024
-
Page
4
On September 7,
1993, the Commission repealed former
Regulation 18703 and adopted a new regulation which established
specific standards for the “public generally” exception.
New
Regulation 18703(a)
provides that the “public generally” exception
applies where both subdivisions
(1)
and
(2)
apply.
(1)
Significant Segment:
The governmental
decision will affect a “significant segment” of the
public generally as set forth below:
(A)
The decision will affect:
(1)
Ten percent or more of the
population in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents,
or
(ii)
Ten percent or more of all
property owners, all home owners,
or all
households in the jurisdiction of the
official’s agency or the district the
official represents,
or
(iii)
Fifty percent of all
businesses in the jurisdiction or the
district the official represents,
so long
as the segment is composed of persons
other than a single industry, trade,
or
profession; or,
(B)
The decision will affect 5,000
individuals who are residents of the
jurisdiction; or,
***
(2)
Substantially the Same Manner:
The
governmental decision will affect the official’s
economic interest in substantially the same manner
as it will affect the economic interests of the
segment identified in subdivision
(a) (1)
of this
regulation.
You indicated that the application in question is specific to
the Tropics Mobile Home Park.
Thus,
only persons in the Tropics
Mobile Home Park would be affected in substantially the same
manner.
According to your facts, the Tropics Mobile Home Park
contains only approximately three percent of the dwelling units in
the city.
We assume that the Tropics Mobile Home Park does not
contain 10 percent of the city’s home owners or population.
Thus,
subdivision
(a)
of Regulation 18703 wou1d not apply.


Our File No.
A-94-024
Page
5
However, Regulation 18703.3 provides:
(a)
For the purposes of Government Code
Section 87103,
the “public generally” exception
applies to appointed members of boards and
commissions who are appointed to represent a
specific economic interest,
as specified in Section
87103(a)
through (d), if all of the following
apply:
(1)
The statute,
ordinance,
or other
provision of law which creates or authorizes
the creation of the board or commission
contains a finding and declaration that the
persons appointed to the board or commission
are appointed to represent and further the
interests of the specific economic interest.
(2)
The member is required to have the
economic interest the member represents.
(3)
The board’s or commission’s decision
does not have a material financial effect on
any other economic interest held by the
member,
other than the economic interest the
member was appointed to represent.
(4)
The decision of the board or
commission will financially affect the
member’s economic interest in a manner that is
substantially the same or proportionately the
same as the decision will financially affect a
significant segment of the persons the member
was appointed to represent.
(b)
In the absence of an express finding and
declaration of the type describedin subdivision
(a) (1)
of this regulation,
the “public generally”
exception only applies if such a finding and
declaration is implicit, taking into account the
language of the statute,
ordinance,
or other
provision of law creating or authorizing the
•creation of the board or commission,
the nature and
purposes of the program,
any applicable legislative
history,
and any other relevant circumstance.
The Union City Municipal Code provides that persons appointed
to the board are appointed to represent and further the interests
of tenants and mobile home park owners.
Commissioner Matthews was
appointed to represent mobile home park tenants and was required
to be a tenant of a mobile home park.
(Union City Municipal Code
Section 16.40.050.)


Our File No.
A-94-024
Page
6
Additionally,
the rent increase before the board will affect
544 of the 933 mobile home units in the city
(58 percent of the
mobile homes in the city)
in substantially the same or
proportionately the same manner as the decision will affect the
official.
4
Thus, Regulation 18703.3 applies,
and Commissioner Matthews
may participate in the decision.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—59Ol.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
Geriral Counsel
ohn W.
Wallace
ounsel,
Legal Division
Please note that since the commissioner was appointed to
represent mobile home park tenants,
the number of tenants
similarly affected is the true test under Regulation 18703.3.
You
have not provided information with respect to the number of
tenants in the Tropics Mobile Home Park.
Absent facts to the
contrary,
we assume for purposes of this letter that the
proportion of units in the commissioner’s park reflects a
reasonable estimate of the proportion of tenants affected.
If
this is not the case,
you should contact us for further advice.
5
.
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively,
copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of
l0cz per page.
